caption: Fig. 1 e The effects of Mn and Al addition on phase constituent and fields of the FeeC system [31e35]: (a) FeeC, (b) Fee15MneC, (c) Fee7AleC. The diashed line in (c) indicates the A2 to B2 transition temperature caused by Al addition.

Fig. 2 e The distribution of k-carbide at various locations [40]. (a, b) k\* -carbide precipitated along grain boundary; (c, d)′ k -carbide precipitated within grain; (e, f) k-carbide precipitated simultaneously along grain boundary and within grain.
citation_sentences: The room temperature microstructure of single austenite FeeMneAleC steels is composed of equiaxed austenite together with annealing twins [39], because the temperature of thermal-mechanical processing is generally located within the single austenite phase region. After cold rolling, the severe plastic deformation makes numerous grains elongated into lath-like morphology along the rolling direction. Due to the differences in element contents and cooling rates, the carbide types formed in austenite are k-carbide and B2/DO3. Generally, k-carbide can be divided into intragranular k0 -carbides and intergranular k\* -carbides, as shown in Fig. 2 [40].

caption: Fig. 3 e Abstract images of hot and cold rolling, annealing and aging processes of FeeMneAleC steel.
citation_sentences: Due to the different element contents in various FeeMneAleC low-density steels, the thermal-mechanical processing technologies, including plastic deformation and heat treatment, are also different. Generally, the forging ingot is homogenized at 1100e1250 C for 1e3 h, then hot-rolled to 2e5 mm thickness between 1100 and 850 C (Fig. 3). In some cases, it needs to reheat among different rolling-passes to avoid crack generation during hot rolling. A large rolling reduction can provide enough recrystallization driving force, thus inhibiting the segregation of Fe Al or FeAl. For austenite-base FeeMneCeAl steels [19,43], the hot-rolled steels can be rapidly cooled to room temperature and then subjected to isothermal annealing and aging treatments (Route 1 in Fig. 3). However, the d-ferrite in hot-rolled steels presents coarse and lath-like morphologies. Thus, cold rolling and recrystallization annealing are employed to tailor the d-ferrite size, texture and k-carbide (Route 2 in Fig. 3). Moreover, the cooling rate after hot rolling strongly influences intergranular k-carbide formation [44]. Note that aging at 500e700 C for 0.5e2 h (Routes 3 and 4 in Fig. 3) is beneficial to the nucleation of nano precipitates.

caption: Fig. 4 e Vickers hardness evolution of 30%, 40% and 50% cold-rolled steels for different annealing times of Fe-22.5Mn-1.2Al-0.3C steel [47].
citation_sentences: For FeeMneCeAl low-density steels, the generation of deformation twins during cold rolling can effectively refine grains through the dynamic Hall-Petch effect to enhance strength [45]. However, the large cold rolling deformation also results in high residual stress, which harms the formability of steels [46]. The annealing process can eliminate work hardening and improve the toughness. Therefore, the combination of cold rolling and subsequent annealing process should be a reliable method to obtain high strength and good formability. Haase et al. [47] studied the influence of cold-rolling reduction (30%, 40% and 50%) and annealing time on the hardness of a Fee22.5Mne1.2Ale0.3C steel. The hardness was improved with the increase of cold rolling reduction due to the high work hardening but was decreased with the increase of annealing time due to recrystallization (Fig. 4). Zhang et al. [48] designed the A-BDP steel with ultrafine grains by adopting austenite recrystallization and ferrite transformation via annealing in a cold rolled Fee25.7Mne10.6Ale1.2C steel. The final microstructure consisted of recrystallized austenite, recovered austenite, ultrafine ferrite and nano k-carbide, and the average grain size reached $1 . 1 2 \pm 0 . 5 7 ~ \mu \mathrm { m }$ . An et al. [49] adopted a simple thermomechanical method of cold rolling and subsequent annealing to tailor the grain size and B2 particles in a Fee28Mne11Ale1Ce5Ni steel. Nano B2 particles precipitated along the recrystallized austenite grain boundaries, while the ultrafine B2 particles were generated in coarse non-recrystallized austenite. Such a complex microstructure can significantly overcome the strength-ductility trade-off, but a few nano precipitates limit the enhancement of yield strength.

caption: Fig. 5 e (a) Contributed yield strength increment of alloying elements in austenite steel [6]; (b) Engineering stress-strain curves of samples with various austenite grain sizes [48].
citation_sentences: Austenite-base FeeMneAleC steels contain a large fraction of Al, Mn and C elements. Interstitial C and N atoms enter into FCC g-Fe, the lattice distortion is caused and the stress field is formed simultaneously, which then interacts with dislocations in the matrix to generate a strong hardening effect. In contrast, the lattice distortion caused by the replacement atoms (such as Mn, Ni or Si) is spherical symmetry, so its strengthening effect is weaker than that of interstitial solid solution atoms [26,56]. The influence of alloy elements on yield strength of austenite steels is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The 1 wt % C contributes to a yield strength increment of 187e300 MPa for austenite, while the contributed yield strength of 1 wt% Al is slightly below 10 MPa. Mn addition can not only improve the austenite fraction but increase the solubility of Al and C in austenite, both of which indirectly affect the mechanical properties.

According to the Hall-Petch effect, grain refinement strengthening is another strengthening mechanism. Generally, cold rolling, recrystallization annealing, and adding micro-alloying elements (Nb, V, Ti, and Mo et al.) are also used to refine grains $[ 5 5 , 5 7 - 5 9 ]$ . Zhang et al. [48] prepared a coldrolled $\mathrm { F e { \mathrm { - } } M n { \mathrm { - } } A l { \mathrm { - } } C }$ dual-phase steel with ultrafine grains in consideration of austenite recrystallization and ferrite transformation. The result implied that the austenite grain refinement increased the yield strength and ultimate tensile strength above 400 MPa (Fig. 5b). However, too small grains will weaken this strengthening effect.

caption: Fig. 6 e (a, b) Lattice of B2 phase and NiAl ordered phases in FeeMneAleC low density steel [69]; (c, d) B2 phase precipitates in aged FeeMneAleC steel [71].
citation_sentences: The k-carbide has L0 12 ordered crystal structure similar to that of perovskite oxide, as shown in Fig. 6 [63]. The chemical formula of k-carbide is usually (Fe, Mn) AlC . Still, the exact chemical composition is unknown because the C content in ultrafine precipitates is challenging to measure. In 1995, Palm [64] reported that the chemical formula of k-carbide should be $( \mathrm { F e , \ M n ) _ { y } A l _ { 1 - y } C _ { x } \ ( - 0 . 2 \ < \ y < + 0 . 2 , \ 0 . 4 2 \ < \ x \ < \ 0 . 7 1 ) }$ through experiment and accurate calculation. Although the commonly used Fe AlC formula does not take into account the Fe/Al ratio deviation, it is still widely used. Seol et al. [42] proposed that the formation of k-carbide was determined by a eutectoid reaction related to nucleation and growth, which further revealed that the composition of k-carbide varied with the aging conditions and alloy composition.

High C (>0.7 wt%) and Al (>7.0 wt) contents are the primary conditions for k-carbide formation in single austenite-base FeeMneAleC steels. However, k-carbide can be divided into k0 -carbide and k\* -carbide according to the aging temperature. When the aging temperature ranges from 500 to $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the spinodal decomposition will occur in austenite, which is described as follows: $\gamma \to \gamma ^ { \prime } + \gamma " \to \gamma " + \mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ (short-range ordering phase) /g'þk' [65]. Meanwhile, the k0 -carbide refers to the ultrafine precipitate nucleated within the austenite matrix (Fig. 6), showing a coherent relationship between them [29,42,61]. Finally, the ultrafine layer k0 -carbide in austenite and nano dispersed k0 -carbide in ferrite can significantly enhance yield stress and cause aging hardening [66,67]. As the aging temperature increases to $7 0 0 { - } 9 0 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the coarse $\kappa ^ { \prime } -$ carbide will generate in austenite matrix or along the prior austenite grain boundary. This k0 -carbide is called intercrystalline k\* -carbide. Therefore, the high aging temperature can accelerate the precipitation of coarse k\* -carbide along the grain boundary. Moreover, the long aging time will lead to the formation of brittle b-Mn phase. However, k\* -carbide and $\beta -$ Mn are unfavorable to the ductility and toughness of FeeMneAleC low-density steels [68]. Therefore, the aging temperature and time should be precisely regulated to avoid the formations of intergranular k\* -carbide and b-Mn in single austenite-base FeeMneAleC steel. Under a plastic deformation, the interaction between k-carbides and dislocations does not change the plane slip mode, but the additional dislocation sources are activated and acted upon by cross slip [38]. Moreover, these nano k-carbides uniformly distribute in austenite and then promote the plane slip. As the strain increases, the plane dislocation structure is further refined, contributing to continuous strain hardening. This is the main contribution of k-carbide strengthening in single austenitebase FeeMneAleC steels.

When the FeeMneAleC low-density steels undergo quenching-aging/cold rolling-annealing, the (Fe, Mn)Al or NiAl type ordered intermetallic compounds are formed. B2 phase is an ordered body-centered cubic structure of CsCl type, as shown in Fig. 6a and b [69]. The previous reports imply that B2 at room temperature is unfavorable to plasticity, but it is favorable to corrosion resistance, oxidation resistance and wear resistance [70].

In addition to k-carbide, a small amount of ultrafine aferrite precipitate along the austenite grain boundary in A-BDP FeeMneAleC steels under different annealing temperatures. High Al content can induce the a-ferrite transformation into B2 and DO3. Kim et al. [71] reported that the Ni addition in FeeMneAleC steels effectively combined with Al to form the NiAl-type B2 phase. Numerous studies implied that cold rolling and annealing processes could provide a suitable condition for B2 nucleation [42,43,53]. After cold rolling and annealing, it was found that there are three different morphologies (banded, blocky and ultrafine) of B2 phases (Fig. 6c) in the Fee16Mn-9.6Al-0.86C-4.9Ni steel [71]. The banded B2 phase was transformed from d-ferrite (white rectangle area). The blocky B2 phase, which precipitated along the recrystallized austenite grain boundaries, was transformed from aferrite (yellow rectangle area). However, the ultrafine B2 particles nucleated on shear bands of recrystallized austenite grains (red rectangle area). The widely distributed B2 phase contributed to the enhancements of yield strength, tensile strength, and ferrite hardness.

Compared with k-carbide, B2 phase is not sheared by slip dislocations. This phenomenon leads to the dislocation pileup at phase boundary, finally enhancing the work hardening of FeeMneAleC steels containing B2 phase during plastic deformation (Fig. 6d) [71]. In this regard, relevant scholars have given different opinions. Yang et al. [72] believed the enhancement of work hardening was attributed to high back stress due to the presence of B2 phase. During plastic deformation, the strain incompatibility between the austenite matrix and B2 phase accelerated the dislocation multiplication at phase interfaces and formed high internal stress, resulting in the retardation of dislocation slip in austenite. At a much larger plastic deformation, B2 phase began to yield and the long-term back stress was formed. However, Kim et al. [73] insisted that the short-range ordering phases were the internal factor responsible for work hardening, rather than the difference in stress/strain distribution in FeeMneAleC steels. Sohn et al. [74] pointed out that the NiAl-type B2 phase precipitated in Ni-containing steels had a stronger chemical bond than FeAl-type B2 precipitates in Ni-free steels, which was conducive to improving strength. However, a large number of hard and brittle B2 phases distributed along the grain boundary would deteriorate the plasticity and toughness.

caption: Fig. 7 e Relationship between composition, deformation mechanism and SFE in FeeMneAleC steel under room temperature [80].
citation_sentences: Al content strongly influences the tensile properties of single ferrite-base FeeMneAleC steels by influencing crystal structures (Fig. 7) [80]. When the Al content reaches 6 wt%, the k phase with a short-range ordering crystal structure is gradually formed, resulting in a sharp decrease of ductility [81]. With the increase of Al content over 7 wt%, the short-range ordering reaction continuously occurs, and the shear action of dislocations on ordered phases induces high work hardening during plastic deformation [82]. Further increasing Al content to a peak of 16 wt%, a terrific amount of B2 and DO3 phases with super crystal structures are formed, resulting in a rapid decrease of work hardening [20]. Herrmann et al. [83] observed dislocation slip bands in Fe-xAl steels, and they believed that the generation and slip of dislocations in a Fee6Al steel determined the deformation mechanism during plastic deformation. As the Al content increased to 11e19 wt %, the number of active mobile dislocations decreased under a low deformation temperature, due to a limited number of dislocation slip systems. At this time, the number of mobile dislocations sensitively depended on crystal orientation. After fracture, the dense dislocation bundles and undeformed B2 phase were also observed in ferrite, indicating that uneven plastic deformation occurred in ferrite. The above discussion implies that the shear of mobile dislocations on ordered phases and dislocation slips mainly control the strain hardening of single ferrite steels.

caption: Fig. 8 e The mechanism of deformation twins near grain boundaries in Fe-17.5Mn-1.5Al-0.58C steel under various strain [86]. (aec) After straining at 0.02, stacking faults and Frank partial dislocations on the (111) plane are indicated by red and orange arrows, respectively; (def) After straining at 0.05, deformation twins formed near the austenite grain boundary and gradually grew into the grains. Red arrows indicate the starting point of twins and a yellow arrow indicates the growth direction of twins in (e).
citation_sentences: Low-density TWIP steels have a high Mn content of 20e30 wt%. After rolling, annealing and quenching treatments, the microstructure consists of complete austenite together with a large number of annealing twins. Hong et al. [86] studied the formation mechanism of deformation twins in a Fee17.5Mne1.5Al-0.85C TWIP steel (Fig. 8). They deemed that the prior austenite grain boundary provided the nucleated locations for deformation twins. When the tensile strain rate reached 0.02, various slip systems were activated near the austenite grain boundary. The interaction among mobile dislocations on different slip planes led to the formation of Shockley and Frank partial dislocations at positions slightly away from the prior austenite grain boundary, finally forming the stacking faults. Further straining to 0.05, deformation twins formed near the austenite grain boundary and gradually grew into the grains. Therefore, the strong dislocation movement and deformation twins systematically contributed to the high strain hardening of TWIP steels.

caption: Fig. 9 e The evolution of dislocation substructures in austenite (aed) and ferrite (e, f) of Fee20Mne9Al-0.6C duplex phase steel under different tensile strains [89].
citation_sentences: Generally, austenite determines the deformation mechanism when the d-ferrite fraction is less than 10%. Frommeyer et al. [85] studied the influence of solution and water-quenching treatments on a Fee26Mne10AleC steel with 883 MPa tensile strength and 65% total elongation. The uniform shear deformation of austenite determined such an outstanding combination of strength and ductility. Moreover, with the increase of dferrite fraction, both ferrite and austenite provided strong strain hardening effect. Hwang et al. [89] studied the evolution of dislocation substructures in austenite and ferrite of a Fee20Mne9Al-0.6C duplex phase steel under different tensile strains. For austenite, the unidirectional slips were observed on the (111) plane under 5% tensile strain (Fig. 9a), and multidirectional slips occurred with the increase of strain, as shown in Fig. 9b and (c). After straining at 40%, unidirectional slips determined the deformation mechanism, while the space of slip line was much smaller than that under low strain (Fig. 9d). For ferrite, dislocations dominated the deformation mechanism at 5% low strain, and explosive dislocation multiplication developed into dislocation cells at 40% high strain (Fig. 9e and f). It can be seen that austenite shows an obvious plane slip mechanism due to the high SFE, while ferrite shows wave slips and forms the dislocation cells.

caption: Fig. 10 e The ln(ds/dε) vs. lns curves of Fee12Mne8Ale0.8C steel shows three stages with different slopes at the critical strains $( \mathbf { { \varepsilon _ { 1 } } }$ and $\mathbf { \epsilon } _ { \mathbf { \mathcal { E } } _ { 2 } } )$ [90].
citation_sentences: The hardness difference between ferrite and austenite in A-BDP steels results in the multi-stage strain hardening behavior during the tensile deformation [90,91]. With the increase of plastic deformation, austenite showed higher strain hardening than ferrite, so the Fee12Mne8Ale0.8C A-BDP steel showed three stages of strain hardening curve (Fig. 10) [90]. In the first stage, the single ferrite deformation led to a sharp decrease in strain hardening rate. In the second stage, austenite started to deform and the strain hardening curve presented approximately a line. In the third stage, the severe strain localization resulted in a quick drop in strain hardening rate, due to the obvious hardness difference between ferrite and austenite.

caption: Fig. 11 e Microstructure of ferrite and austenite of the as quenched-annealed Fee27Mne12Ale0.8C duplex steel before and after tensile deformation [91]. (a) Fine DO3 particles in ferrite; (b) Nano k-carbide in austenite; (c, d) Deformed microstructure of ferrite 5% strain, dislocations inside the B2 domain; (e, f) Deformed microstructure of austenite 5% strain.
citation_sentences: The ordered B2 and DO3 phases are formed on the disordered ferrite matrix of A-BDP steels, which will affect the deformation behavior [91,92]. Park et al. [91] investigated the influences of quenching and ordering annealing on microstructure and deformation mechanism of a Fee27Mne12Ale0.8C A-BDP steel with 27% d-ferrite fraction (Fig. 11). The nano k-carbide also precipitated in austenite under quenching or quenching-ordering annealing conditions, and its size gradually became larger with increasing ordering annealing temperature. Interestingly, the coarse B2 and ultrafine DO3 particles precipitated in ferrite. During tensile deformation, the k-carbide in austenite was sheared by plane slip dislocations; however, the strong interaction among super-dislocations with narrow mechanical reverse domain boundaries occurred in ferrite. Therefore, the strain hardening of A-BDP steels is mainly controlled by the shearing effect of ordered phases by super-dislocations in ferrite and plane slip dislocations in austenite.

caption: Fig. 12 e Strain hardening rate curve (a) and the corresponding strengthening mechanism (bef) of Fee3.9Mne5.8Ale0.35C F-BDP steel under different tensile strains [15]. (bed) Straining at 10%; (e, f) Straining at 20%.
citation_sentences: Similar to A-BDP steels, the strain hardening rate curve of F-BDP steels presents various stages. Liu et al. [15] studied the strain hardening mechanism of a Fee3.9Mne5.8Ale0.35C F-BDP steel under different tensile strains, as shown in Fig. 12. In the first stage, the dislocation motion in ferrite led to a sharp decrease in strain hardening rate. In the second stage, due to the low stability and fraction of austenite, the limited TRIP and TWIP effects at a low strain could not contribute much to strain hardening (Fig. 12bed). The increase of strain hardening rate was mainly attributed to the dislocation slip in ferrite and austenite. Meanwhile, the strain hardening curve reached a peak value and entered a long-term continuous hardening stage. In the third stage, the dislocation overcame obstacles caused by the increased strain in previous stages and the dislocation motion resistance decreased, thus reducing the hardening rate (Fig. 12e and f). In the fourth stage, the martensite transformation rate was rapidly increased, and TWIP effect was also activated with the increase of strain. Therefore, the strain hardening mechanism in this stage included TRIP, TWIP effect and dislocation strengthening. In the fifth stage, the martensite transformation rate significantly decreased with the loss of austenite. Meanwhile, the volume expansion caused by martensite transformation intensified the local non-uniform deformation, resulting in a slow decrease in strain hardening rate.

caption: Fig. 13 e (a) Charpy impact absorbed energy of Fe-xMn-2Al-0.45C-0.05Si steel as a function of Mn content [101]; (b) Charpy impact absorbed energy of Fee18MnexAl-0.6C steel as a function of Al content [102].
citation_sentences: The element content strongly influences cryogenic toughness. Lee et al. [101] pointed out that the (17, 19 and 22 wt%) Mn addition into Fe-xMn-2Al-0.45C low-density steels significantly affected the tensile and Charpy impact properties under cryogenic conditions (Fig. 13a). However, the influences of Mn content on the tensile and Charpy impact properties at room temperature were minimal. Under cryogenic conditions, the ductility and impact energy gradually decreased with the increase of Mn content. The corresponding cryogenic ductility for 17, 19 and 22 wt% Mn steels were 75.7%, 53.4% and 9.7%, respectively. Whereas the corresponding impact energies were 80.5, 49.8 and 41.1 J, respectively. Moreover, Chen et al. [102] investigated the effect of (3, 5 and 8 wt%) Al addition into Fee18MnexAl-0.6C low-density steels on impact energy at cryogenic temperature (Fig. 13b). With the Al content increasing from 3 to 5 wt%, the impact energy at cryogenic temperature increased from 122 to 138 J. However, 8 wt% Al content resulted in a decrease of impact energy at cryogenic temperature.

caption: Fig. 14 e The microstructure evolution in Fee30Mne6.5Ale0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel during tensile deformation at ¡196 C [103].
citation_sentences: The secondary phase transformation is significant for the deterioration of the impact energy at cryogenic temperature. For example, the strain-induced martensite transformation during plastic deformation could relieve the local stress and enhance the impact energy of a $\mathrm { F e } { - } 1 8 \mathrm { M n } { - } 8 \mathrm { A l } { - } 0 . 6 \mathrm { C }$ lowdensity steel [102]. The coarse banded d-ferrite phase boundaries acted as the center of microcrack nucleation and propagation during tensile or impact deformations, finally resulting in the unacceptable degradation of mechanical properties at cryogenic temperature. In addition, the microband structure can also enhance the mechanical properties of FeeMneAleC low-density steels at cryogenic temperature. Gutierrez-Urrutia et al. [103] systemically investigated the microband evolution in a Fee30Mne6.5Ale0.3C (wt.%) lowdensity steel during tensile deformation at 196 C (Fig. 14). Under a low strain, the intense movement of closely spaced slip bands activated the planar slip localization, finally forming the microbands (Fig. 14a and b). At a high strain, the new microbands formed together with the generation of deformation twins (Fig. 14a and b). However, the microbands located within austenite grains were not conducive to the formation of deformation twins (Fig. 14c and d). This complex deformation structure led to the highly uneven deformation behavior, which significantly enhanced the work hardening and mechanical properties of studied steels under cryogenic conditions.

caption: Fig. 15 e (a) Relationship between Charpy impact absorbed energy and testing temperature for FeeMneAleC austenite steel [104,105]. (b) Relationship between impact toughness and Al content for FeeMneAleC steel [106].
citation_sentences: Al content also affects the impact toughness of aged FeeMneAleC steels (Fig. 15b) [106]. This phenomenon is closely related to k-carbide precipitation and d-ferrite formation. When Al content was lower than 7%, k-carbide could not precipitate, but $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 7 }$ and ${ \bf M } _ { 7 } { \bf C } _ { 3 }$ might precipitate along grain boundaries during aging, reducing the impact toughness. After increasing Al content to 7%e10%, the formations of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 7 }$ and ${ \bf M } _ { 7 } { \bf C } _ { 3 }$ were inhibited, but the brittle k-carbide gradually precipitated along the grain boundary, also leading to a decreased impact toughness. When Al content exceeded 10%, d-ferrite gradually formed and further reduced the impact toughness. Due to the deformation incompatibility between austenite and ferrite phases, microcracks were mainly generated at the phase interface.

caption: Fig. 16 e The fatigued surface and atom force microscopy images (a) and slip band structure (b) in Fee16Mne2Ale0.3C steel [109]. The deformed microstructures of micro-alloyed Fee28Mne7Ale1C steel after fatigue failure at 0.4% (c, d) and 0.8% (e) strains [111].
citation_sentences: Hamada et al. [109] systematically investigated the fatigue behavior of high-Mn steels with various grain sizes. They insisted that the decreased grain size enhanced the fatigue resistance, and the cracks preferentially generated at sites of intersections of slip bands, grain boundaries, and annealing twin boundaries. However, the continuous slip bands were generated and deformation twins were absent during highcycle straining [109]. Subsequently, they analyzed the cyclic behaviors of a Fee16Mne1.5Ale0.3C steel with fine- and coarse-grained structures [110]. In the coarse-grained steel, deformation twins, stacking faults, planar dislocation arrays, and dislocation tangles were observed under cyclic loading. Still, the deformation twins were only displayed in the finegrained steel (Fig. 16a and b). It can be seen that the fatigue behavior of FeeMneAleC steels are closely related to chemical compositions and microstructures. Zhao et al. [111] pointed out that the microalloyed element additions in FeeMneAleC steels exhibited a higher fatigue life than that of non-microalloyed one. Numerous parallel deformation bands together with planar dislocations and nano-size precipitates showed a positive influence on enhancing the fatigue life (Fig. 16cee). The previous reports mainly focus on the effect of various deformation mechanisms on fatigue behavior of FeeMneAleC steels. However, the influence of micro-alloyed carbides on the cyclic behavior of FeeMneAleC steels is reported minimally.

caption: Fig. 17 e Hydrogen-induced intergranular crack in Fee26Mne11Ale1.2C austenitic steel containing k-carbides [113]. (a) Inverse pole figure. (b) KAM figure. (c) Orientation-optimized ECC image corresponding to the part surrounded by the red broken lines in (b).
citation_sentences: Hydrogen embrittlement is the hydrogen dissolved in steels, which causes stress concentration and then forms microcracks, resulting in catastrophic fracture of the structural material and component [112e115]. Once hydrogen embrittlement is generated, it can not be eliminated. FeeMneAleC low-density steels are considered as high-strength steels for industrial applications. It is necessary to study the hydrogen embrittlement sensitivity and its influence on mechanical properties. Koyama et al. [112] insisted that the fracture modes induced by hydrogen embrittlement were mainly composed of intergranular and transgranular fractures in a Fee18Mne1.2C steel. Meanwhile, the transgranular fracture was initiated parallel to the primary and secondary deformation twin boundaries, resulting in the generation of cracks at grain boundaries and twin boundaries. Afterwards, they examined the hydrogen-assisted cracking mechanisms of a Fee26Mne11Ale1.2C steel [113]. They proposed that the grain boundary triple junction cracking and slip-localizationinduced intergranular cracking along micro-voids determined the embrittlement ability. Meanwhile, grain boundary triple junction cracking was preferentially generated on grain boundaries, while the microscopically ductile sliplocalization-induced intergranular cracking assisted crack growth during plastic deformation, resulting in the macroscopic brittle fracture appearance (Fig. 17).
Fig. 17(c)

caption: Fig. 18 e Microstructure (aed) and wear resistance (e) of Fee25.1Mne6.6Ale1.3C steel under quehching at 1050 C and aging at 550 C for 2 and 4 h [117].
citation_sentences: Due to the interaction among various strengthening mechanisms, FeeMneCeAl low-density steels show good wear performance. Sohrabizadeh et al. [116] evaluated a relationship between aging time and wear rate in a Fe-22.33Mn-1.35C-9.87Al steel. After aging at high temperature or for long duration, the k-carbide within austenite matrix could inhibit crack nucleation during the wear test. Peng et al. [117] selected a quenched Fee25Mne1.3Ce6.6Al steel upon aging at 550 C for different time (from 1 to 4 h) to conduct impact abrasive wear tests (Fig. 18). The fine nano-size k-carbide precipitated coherently within the austenite matrix under the aging treatment for 2 h (Fig. 18a), which resulted in the best wear resistance and hardness (Fig. 18e). However, a longer aging time resulted in the decomposing of austenite into k-carbide toward the whole austenite grains and grain boundaries (Fig. 18b). These over-coarsened k-carbide along grain boundaries made the material extremely brittle, decreasing the wear resistance (Fig. 18e). The surface hardening showed LomereCottrell locks, high-density dislocation tangles, and dislocation walls; neither martensite nor deformation twins were observed under the low impact energy condition (Fig. 18c and d). It can be seen that the aging time play an essential role in the wear resistance of FeeMneCeAl low-density steels. The suitable aging time and temperature should be further studied.

caption: Fig. 19 e The measured room-temperature tensile properties of FeeMneAleC low-density steels with various Mn contents [15,23,34,51,52,79,80], [91e93], [120e150].
